examples.title: Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle
examples.description: Demonstrates how the CPU processes a single instruction through the four phases
examples.code:
// FETCH PHASE
PC = PC + 1                    // Increment Program Counter
MAR = PC                       // Move address to Memory Address Register
READ(MAR)                      // Read instruction from memory
MDR = MEMORY[MAR]              // Store instruction in Memory Data Register
IR = MDR                       // Load instruction into Instruction Register

// DECODE PHASE
OPCODE = IR[15:12]             // Extract operation code
OPERAND = IR[11:0]             // Extract operand address
CONTROL_SIGNALS = DECODE(OPCODE)  // Determine required operations

// EXECUTE PHASE
IF OPCODE == ADD THEN
    MAR = OPERAND
    READ(MAR)                  // Fetch operand from memory
    ACC = ACC + MDR            // Perform addition
ELSE IF OPCODE == STORE THEN
    MAR = OPERAND
    MDR = ACC
    WRITE(MAR)                 // Store result to memory
END IF

// STORE PHASE
// Result already stored in ACC or memory during execute
PC = PC + 1                    // Prepare for next instruction
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows how the CPU processes each instruction. The FETCH phase retrieves the instruction, DECODE interprets what operation to perform, EXECUTE performs the actual computation, and STORE saves the result. The cycle repeats for each instruction.

examples.title: Interrupt Handling Mechanism
examples.description: Shows how the CPU handles an interrupt and transfers control to the interrupt service routine
examples.explanation: This demonstrates how interrupts are handled. When an interrupt occurs, the CPU automatically saves the current state, switches to kernel mode, executes the appropriate interrupt service routine, and then restores the interrupted program to continue execution.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of registers to save

examples.title: System Call Interface Example
examples.description: Demonstrates how a user program makes a system call to read data from a file
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates system calls in practice. The key point is that user programs cannot directly access hardware; they must request OS services through system calls. The transition from user mode to kernel mode is triggered by the 'syscall' instruction.

examples.title: DMA Transfer Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates how Direct Memory Access allows data transfer without CPU intervention
examples.code:
import time

class DMAController:
    """Simulates a DMA controller managing data transfer"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.memory = [0] * 1000  # Simulated main memory
        self.device_buffer = []   # Simulated I/O device buffer
        
    def cpu_initiate_dma(self, source_address, dest_address, count):
        """CPU sets up DMA transfer - quick setup phase"""
        print(f"CPU: Setting up DMA transfer of {count} bytes")
        print(f"      From device buffer to memory address {dest_address}")
        
        # CPU configures DMA controller (very fast operation)
        dma_config = {
            'source': 'device',
            'dest': dest_address,
            'count': count,
            'mode': 'read'  # Read from device
        }
        
        # CPU tells DMA to start and resumes other work
        print("CPU: DMA configured, resuming other tasks...")
        return dma_config
    
    def dma_transfer(self, config):
        """DMA controller handles transfer independently"""
        print("DMA: Starting transfer...")
        
        # Simulate data transfer from device to memory
        for i in range(config['count']):
            # Read from device (simulated)
            data = self.device_buffer[i] if i < len(self.device_buffer) else i
            
            # Write directly to memory
            self.memory[config['dest'] + i] = data
            
            # Small delay to simulate hardware timing
            time.sleep(0.01)
        
        print(f"DMA: Transfer of {config['count']} bytes completed")
        print("DMA: Sending interrupt to CPU...")
        
        return True
    
    def cpu_process_completion(self):
        """CPU processes DMA completion interrupt"""
        print("CPU: Processing DMA completion interrupt")
        print("CPU: Data is now available in memory!")

# Demonstrate DMA operation
print("=== DMA Transfer Demonstration ===\n")

# Initialize
dma = DMAController()
dma.device_buffer = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]  # Simulated device data

# Without DMA - CPU would be blocked
print("--- Without DMA ---")
print("CPU would be busy for entire transfer duration")
print("\n--- With DMA ---")

# CPU initiates DMA and continues working
config = dma.cpu_initiate_dma(source_address=0, dest_address=100, count=5)
print("CPU: Doing other useful work while DMA transfers...")
print("CPU: Processing some calculations...")

# DMA transfers independently
dma.dma_transfer(config)

# CPU processes completion
dma.cpu_process_completion()

print("\n=== Transfer Complete ===")
examples.explanation: This Python simulation demonstrates DMA operation. The key benefit is that the CPU initiates the transfer, then continues with other tasks while the DMA controller handles the actual data movement independently. Only when the transfer completes does the CPU need to process the completion interrupt.
